sentences.text: Yes, I went for a little while.
I can't-- I can't recall what happened.
Did we--

No, but I think it became a problem with-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- it was-- I think after coming back, I had a hard time catching up, because I had to learn Russian and I had to learn Yiddish.
I had to learn things that were new to me.
I think I went to-- to another public school for a little while.
And I didn't do well at all.
[SIGHS] Then summer came.
And there were vacations.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and then the war started with- with the with the Germans.
And the Germans came over.
And there was no-- no school then, at all.
[SIGHS]
When the Germans came?
Yes.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I- I- I don't know.
I knew that the- the war started and a lot of people were hurt, because we were right on the border.
But I- I didn't know-- I wasn't frightened of them.
I didn't know, except for what people were saying.
Some were saying they're better than the Russians.
Some where saying they're worse than the Russians.
Most people trusted them, because they knew the stories of- of Germans from the First World War.
And they thought they were civilized and they can be trusted.
And people were overdoing by telling stories about them.
I had mixed emotions, but when you have your parents, and you have a loving home, you feel protected, until we were told to go to the ghetto-- just take as little as possible and go to the ghetto.
And when we came to the ghetto, we shared-- the ghetto was in the middle of town, with barbed wire around a certain part of town, close to a river.
And this is where all the Jews had to settle.
So most of the time, everybody got just either part of a room or a room, if they were a larger family.
We had one room.
And our grandmother was with us.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And we shared a kitchen with other people.
Everybody was trying to be as clean as possible, because they were all put together.
Do you remember how many Jews there were in your town?
I don't remember, but I heard that there were 25,000 [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Jews.
I don't know if this is in the ghetto, because they brought people in from smaller towns, or there were all the Jews from our our town.
It was Wlodzimierz, the same--
Yeah.
This is what I heard.
But I- I have no-- no knowledge of how many Jews were there.
No, we went-- in 1939, the war started.
And we- we were under the Russians till '40-- till 1942, I guess.
And as soon as the Germans came into our town, I think the first ghetto-- I'm sorry.
I don't remember any dates whatsoever.
But I remember that we went to the ghetto.
And after a while in the ghetto, there was the first pogrom.
They rounded up everybody, put them on trucks.
People who could were hiding.
People who could were running away.
People who- who knew somebody had gone to farmers.
They-- but they rounded up most woman, children, sick people, and old people-- put them on big trucks and took them out of town, where big graves were dug by Jews prior to that.
And they were told the digging was for oil tanks.
But they didn't know it was their own graves they were digging.
And there were three huge graves like that.
They're still there-- huge graves.
And--
It was out of town.
It was, I would guess, a few kilometers out of town.
And they were lining them up like sardines, on both sides of the graves, and then shooting one, who fell with their heads this way, and the others over that way.
They told everybody to take their clothes off, to take their jewelry off, and throw them in a certain pile.
And certain girls that were really good looking and beautiful were spared.
They told them to go away, to the side.
Most of the others were shot.
Yeah, my mother, who had the two children, and my grandmother were in one of the trucks that went with the first pogrom.
And they were shot.
And me and my father were outside the ghetto working.
Actually, I wasn't supposed to be, but he wanted me to go so I could help him bring some food home.
Because you were in the fields, you could bring potatoes home or exchange something for milk, and bring for the children.
So I was out of the ghetto with him.
And that's how we survived.
Immediately, people were coming from town.
We were in the fields outside of town.
Immediately, everybody knew about it.
[SIGHS] And to confirm that they not there, my father told me to go in to the ghetto the next day and check.
I remember walking in to town.
And the main street was-- there were a lot of dead people lying on Main Street.
I remember going through-- into the ghetto.
I was numb and scared going in and it looked like one big feather factory.
Everything was in feathers.
Because apparently, people who were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] looking for for things were cutting pillows, and cutting bedding, to look for gold or for money.
Those were-- they were raiding it.
Those were civilians.
That had nothing to do with the Germans, I think.
But the whole-- there-- there was a whole-- it looked like a big hurricane would go through - through the thing.
And there were a lot of police in the ghetto.
There were Germans in the ghetto.
And I went in.
I had a basket with me, with some bread and- and milk.
And I walked into the place where we lived.
There was nobody there and everything was in shambles.
Everything was thrown around and broken.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I turned around and I went back to tell my father about it.
But when I came back, I couldn't talk.
I just couldn't say anything.
I was absolutely numb till the lady, who was a friend of ours, a Polish lady, came up.
And she start hitting me in the face-- not out of hatred, just -just sort of wake me up, I think.
And I told my father what I saw.
And he broke down.
She kept telling me that my father had a nervous breakdown, that he's not-- he's not capable of making any decisions.

But she said because I didn't look Jewish, and I looked very much like one of her children, I should just go away from here, where nobody knows me, and go on with a normal life, as if-- as if nothing happened.
And when all of this is finished, you'll come back, and your father will be here.
She made arrangements with her two nephews, that they would come and pick me up in the morning.
She told me what to say.
She told me to say that I have a bad stepmother who's beating me and that I'm running away from home and I need a job, any job, and-- even looking after the cows or- or horses.
And I'll work for anything.
I'll work for food, as long as I don't have to go back to my grand-- to my stepmother.
So she made the arrangements.
And the boys came in the morning.
And she told my father that I'm better off that way because nobody will know.
And she told the boys to start early in the morning-- it was still dark outside-- until sundown.
Doesn't matter where it is.
At sundown, they should just tell me to go, and turn around and come back.
And they did.
But when I went into the first house, and-- they said they'll just wait.
If I stay in the house, it means I got the job.
If I come out from the house, it means [BACKROUND NOISE] they have to go further with me.
So I went into that house.
And I ask if she can give me a job.
And she says, well my husband isn't home, so just wait.
When he'll come back, I'll ask him.
And I didn't know what to do.
So I waited.
And the boys realized that I didn't come out.
They turned around and went back home.
And when her husband came, he said, no way can she have somebody else in the house.
They don't have enough to eat.
So he said I should go.
By that time, it was dark and it was nighttime.
And I-- I was scared.
I didn't know where to go.
So I ask if I could stay overnight and maybe she can find a job for me somewhere.
So I stayed with her overnight.
I actually-- wherever she went, I went with her, because I was scared of her husband.
And she had two children I was playing with, little ones.
And in the morning, [SIGHS] she said she's going to find out, maybe a friend of hers would give me a job.
And she went with me.
And this friend told her that there is somebody else who would give me a job.
They are rich, because they were half German.
The woman was Polish and the man was a Volksdeutsch, which is a German.
And they had more land.
And they had more cows, because they were favored.
So she went to them.
They were older people.
And she got me a job with them.
So it turned out OK.
And I worked for them quite a while.
[SIGHS] As I-- Were they nice people?
They were-- they were-- I mean, they weren't extremely nice to me, but they were whatever they were.
They didn't do me any harm, as long as I did what I did.
But I had a lot to do.
And for somebody who didn't know how to do those things, it was double hard, because I had to learn and pretend that I knew it.
I wasn't going to pretend that I never done that thing.
So I would be extremely careful how I was doing.
I was extremely anxious to do it well and to do more than necessary so they would keep me there.
Yeah, their name was Winters.
They had married children.
And they had grandchildren.
Their children didn't identify themselves with the German background.
They were all Polish.
They married Polish people.
And they identified themselves with the Poles.
Mr.
Winters, who spoke German, had many friends in the German occupational army.
He was making homebrew.
And they were coming and drinking.
Yes, all the time.
I- I wouldn't call it friends, but she was my superior.
She told me what to do and I did it.
She was never affectionate to me.
She - she trusted me to the point where one day she called me in.
And she told me that she's going to tell me a secret.
And that secret meant that if I can keep it, I'll stay alive.
And if I can't keep that secret, I might die, because she has two Jewish men hiding in the barn.
In-- in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was straw, a barn full of straw.
And inside the straw, in a hole that they dug for themselves, there were the two Jewish men.
And from now on, I'm supposed to bring the food to them.
Her husband doesn't know about it.
And he can't be trusted to know.
Because they gave her some things, material things, and she keeps them for that reason.
But if he would know and he would get drunk, he would tell the Germans, who were there all the time.
As it turned out, this was probably a very safe place for them to be, because nobody would think that they were there.
She took me one day when he wasn't there and asked them to come out.
They came out.
And she said to them that-- that I'm working for her now and told them to-- she repeated in front of them.
She says, I told her that if she's going to mention to anybody that you're staying here, that you're going to kill her.
You have nothing to lose, right?
Because either way, you're going to be killed.
But you'll make sure that she goes with you, right?
And they said, right.
[SIGHS]
So I was very surprised.
And I was very confused.
I didn't know if I should tell them who I am, so they wouldn't be scared of me, or I heard stories at church all the time that when they said, once you catch a Jew, he tells you about the other Jews.
And by now, there are none, because once you punish them, they tell you where other Jews are.
They know where they are.
So I took this into consideration, that if you punish somebody, maybe they'll give it out.
And it wasn't fair for them to treat me different.
If they knew I was Jewish, maybe they'll be more familiar, and then she'll find out that I am Jewish.
It was something I was not sleeping over quite a few nights, thinking what was the right thing to do.
And then I decided, for everybody's protection, I'm not going to tell them.
So they called me like, young lady.
In German, they say fraulein, or in Polish, panienka.
They didn't call me by my name at all.
What name were you using?
Do you remember?
Fela.
But she was calling me Felicia, which is like the full name.
But they never called me by my name, they just-- young lady, they would refer to me.
They would come out very early in the morning, when I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] getting up to milk the cows and to feed the horses and the two older people were sleeping in the house.
And they would come out for fresh air.
And they would actually help me with the work.
I was cutting straw for- for feeding the things.
They would help me with that.
Or if I had to carry something heavy, they would help me with that.

Or they would just come into the barn, when I was milking the cow, standing in-- in the corner, so in case somebody comes in, they shouldn't be seen, and talk to me, tell me about their families, and about their wives, who- who were dead by then.
They were two brother-in-laws, married to two sisters.
They told me that there were four sisters-- two were married to them.
And they died with their children.
Another two, who were still young girls, are hiding in a place.
But the guy who is keeping them doesn't mind to keep them, but he hasn't got enough food for them.
So there is a problem.
I suggested to them that since we have more than enough food, they can take some over there, and nobody will ever notice it.
So they would carry extra bread that I would give them to the two sister-in-laws.
I think not only did they want to help them out, but it was somebody they can talk to.
They needed to talk to, after sitting, the two of them, in a small place a whole day long, they would once in a while walk there at night, and- and just have somebody to talk to.
And this was going on for a long time, till spring came, and most of the straw was gone.
And she was scared, so she told them to go.
And I was very sorry to see them go.
They were my light in a dark tunnel.
I was hoping that one day, when-- when the war is finished and I can tell them who I am, because I- I - I was positive that they didn't suspect anything.
Because one day, when it was, they told me, Passover, which I didn't know, and I shouldn't bring them any bread.
And I offered to bring them potatoes that I was boiling in a huge boiler for for the pigs.
And I made sure that I got some oil for them.
And I brought that for them.
They were so moved that one of them said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They-- they were terribly moved that I did extra things for them.
And they kept promising me that after the war, they're going to give me bedding, if I get married.
And they have clothes from their wives that they're going to give me.
And after the war, they're going to really look after me.
And I was thinking that wait and find out who I am.
This is the only thing that I was looking forward to.
But when spring came, and they had to go away, I didn't hear them being killed, but I didn't know where they went.
[SIGHS] The story actually has a bit of an ending, because after the war, I met their two sister-in-laws.
[SIGHS]
The Russians kept coming and going back.
And the Germans kept coming and going back.
They were going forth and back, a few times.
And the Russians were picking up young girls to go to [INAUDIBLE], which is coal mines.
And the lady I worked for wanted to protect me from going.
She needed me around.
So she found me a job in the city.
And she would come every Sunday.
I would go with her to church.
And after church, she would go back home.
And while we were walking from church, I heard, in front of us, people speaking Jewish.
And I just couldn't believe that anybody is alive except me.
And I didn't know that there are other Jews.
And I got very emotional about it.
And I didn't know what to do.
And when she went home, I went to the place that they went into it, which was a broken house, with no windows.
And when I came to the door, a older lady opened the door.
And I tried to say it in Yiddish, but I couldn't.
So I said in Polish, you know, I'm Jewish.
And I kept saying, you know, I'm Jewish.
And she looked at me like I was crazy.
And I was very choked up.
And I kept saying, I'm really Jewish, you know.
So other people came to the door.
And they said, what's going on here?
And she says, oh, she thinks it's so good for us already that she wants to be Jewish.
Look at her.
She keeps saying she's Jewish.
And they said, well, let her in.
So they let me in.
And they asked me where I come from and who I am.
And I told them.
And they said, well where-- how did you stay here, close by?
And I said, I was working for those and those people.
And one of the ladies called to another lady.
And she says, come here and listen to this.
Remember, I told you, she must've been Jewish?
It turned out that those were the two sister-in-laws of the men who stayed in our farm.
And they kept saying to those men, why would she give you all those things if she wasn't Jewish?
And he said, no, she for sure isn't Jewish.
So maybe she was a maid in a Jewish home, where she has some feelings for it.
Says, no, she was too young to be a maid.
So why would she do those things?
And says, she's just a stupid little girl who believes us that, you know, we'll give her one day something.
And she's maybe good natured.
Who knows why she's doing those things?
So she says, we were right.
OK.
